markdown:
## Physical Prototypes for Development


#### What Physical Prototypes Help Designers Test


| Factor | Purpose of Testing | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Scale | Assess whether the size and proportions are appropriate | Holding a model smartphone to test thumb reach |
| Aesthetics | Explore surface finish, shape, and colour appeal | Reviewing car dashboard texture and trim colour |
| Materials | Trial structural integrity, flexibility, texture, or sustainability | Testing 3D-printed casing vs. injection moulded ABS |
| Function | Check how the product operates or performs tasks | Building a prototype door handle to test the mechanism |
| Performance | Evaluate durability, wear, comfort, or real-world reliability | Using a prototype running shoe for test wear over 2 weeks |

<callout type="note">* Physical prototypes are not just about testing functionality. 
* They also help designers understand how a product will be perceived and used in the real world.</callout>

### Types of Physical Prototypes


#### Mock Up


<definition term="Mock-up">A rough or **basic model**, often made from **inexpensive materials**, used to explore **form or function** quickly.</definition>

1. Typically used for** **testing **size, proportions, ergonomics**, and **basic layout**.
2. Quick to build using **cheap or recycled materials** (e.g. cardboard, foam)
3. Prioritises **speed over accuracy**
4. Encourages **early feedback and iteration**
5. May be full-scale or rough approximation

![Mock-up of a Laparoscopic instrument handle (Bernero, 2019)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ea061a2cc1d90d7c3a20ea0a6ba0f5c2410d4ca1-1366x768.png)

#### Scale Model


<definition term="Scale Model">A proportionally **scaled-down (or up)** version of the product, often non-functional.</definition>

1. Typically used to test proportions, spatial relationships, and overall design.
2. Maintains **proportions**, but not size.
3. Often used when full-size models are impractical (e.g. architecture, vehicle design).
4. Can help stakeholders **visualise spatial layout.**
5. May be static or partially interactive.

![Scale model of an building development (Cheung, 2016)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/e6f66389bdeafcee3652daf5e15cf04547563a3c-1200x800.png)

#### Aesthetic Model


<definition term="Aesthetic Model">A high-fidelity, **non-functional** model used to represent the **final appearance**.</definition>

1. Typically used for evaluating **colour, finish, form**, and **brand appeal**.
2. High visual detail but **non-functional.**
3. Used in **presentations, stakeholder approval**, or branding review.
4. Materials chosen to replicate final **look and feel.**
5. Typically built in **later stages **of development

![Aesthetic model of a Skoda car](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5875bbbe935d4c54a7ecbb73492cfd731a2d2209-2000x760.png)

#### Instrumented Model


<definition term="Instrumented Models">A functional prototype fitted with sensors or measurement tools to collect data.</definition>

1. Typically used to measure **performance**, e.g. **stress, temperature, movement**.
2. Includes **sensors or monitoring tools.**
3. Provides **quantitative data** (e.g. force, speed, temperature).
4. Used in **performance testing **or to validate simulation results.
5. Can be expensive or require technical integration

![Instrumented model of a crash dummy (Phan, 2023)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/1b9aae6a08c3f87d6ada949fddce1f7134f2a97b-3600x2200.png)

<callout type="note">Physical prototypes are often created in **multiple stages**, increasing in fidelity, to refine the product based on **real-world testing and feedback**.</callout>

<callout type="analogy">* Think of a physical prototype as a dress rehearsal for a play. 
* It allows the team to see how everything comes together, identify any issues, and make adjustments before the final performance.</callout>

### The Limitations of Physical Prototypes


1. **Cost and Time**
  1. Building physical prototypes can be **expensive** and **time-consuming**, especially for **complex designs**.
2. **Limited Scope**
  1. A single prototype may not capture all aspects of a design, requiring multiple iterations.
3. **Material Constraints**
  1. Prototypes may use **different materials** than the final product, affecting **accuracy** in testing.

<callout type="warning">* Assuming that a single prototype can address all design issues. 
* In reality, multiple prototypes are often needed to test different aspects of a product.</callout>

<example_question>
        <question>
        Explain why prototypes would be used in the testing and evaluation of a new insulated travel mug. [3]
        </question>
        <answer>
        Award [1] for each of three distinct points, up to [3] total. For example:

* **Thermal performance testing**: Prototypes allow measurement of how long the mug keeps drinks hot or cold under real conditions. ✓
* **Leak and seal evaluation**: Early models can be filled, shaken, or tipped to check that the lid and seals prevent spills. ✓
* **Ergonomic feedback**: User trials reveal issues with handle shape, grip comfort, or lid operation that guide refinements. ✓

_(Any equivalent reasons for using prototypes in testing and evaluation of the travel mug.)_
        </answer>
      </example_question>
